docs: This method returns the beginning of bain hashmashos according to the Yereim (Rabbi Eliezer of Metz) calculated as the sun's position 2.1° above the horizon around the equinox / equilux in Yerushalayim, its position 13.5 minutes or 3/4 of an 18-minute mil before sunset. According to the Yereim, bain hashmashos starts 3/4 of a mil before sunset and tzais or nightfall starts at sunset. Details, including how the degrees were calculated can be seen in the documentation of {@link #getBainHashmashosYereim3Point05Degrees()}. @return the Instant of the sun's position 2.1° minutes before sunset. If the calculation can't be computed such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year where the sun does not rise, and one where it does not set, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see ZENITH_MINUS_2_POINT_1 @see #getBainHashmashosYereim13Point5Minutes() @see #getBainHashmashosYereim2Point8Degrees() @see #getBainHashmashosYereim3Point05Degrees()

user_docs.meaning: The beginning of bain hashmashos according to the Yereim, when the sun is 2.1 degrees above the horizon. The docs also describe this as 13.5 minutes, or 3/4 of an 18-minute mil, before sunset.
user_docs.calculation: Calculated from the sun's position 2.1 degrees above the horizon, based on the Yereim's timing of 3/4 of a mil before sunset.
notes: The docs note that this timing is described around the equinox/equilux in Yerushalayim.
If the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set, the zman may not be available.

name: getTzaisGeonim3Point7Degrees
docs: This method returns the tzais (nightfall) based on the opinion of the Geonim calculated at the sun's position at {@link ZENITH_3_POINT_7 3.7°} below {@link GEOMETRIC_ZENITH geometric zenith} (90°), calculated as the position of the sun 13.5 minutes after sunset, the time it takes to walk 3/4 of a mil at 18 minutes a mil, or 13.5 minutes after sunset. The sun is 3.7° below {@link GEOMETRIC_ZENITH geometric zenith} at this time in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux. This does not cover the 26.46 it takes to walk 49 amos (the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi) at the pace of an 18-minute mil. It should be noted that Rabbi Yedidya Manet in his Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh (4th edition part 2, pages and 22 and 24) lists 3.65° that appears to be a drop too early. @return the Instant representing the time when the sun is 3.7° below sea level. @see ZENITH_3_POINT_7

user_docs.meaning: Nightfall according to the Geonim, based on the sun being 3.7 degrees below geometric zenith, which the docs describe as about 13.5 minutes after sunset.
user_docs.calculation: Calculated for the time when the sun is 3.7 degrees below geometric zenith (90 degrees), equivalent in the docs to 13.5 minutes after sunset or the time to walk 3/4 of a mil at 18 minutes per mil.
notes: The docs note that this corresponds to the sun being 3.7 degrees below geometric zenith in Jerusalem around the equinox or equilux.
The docs also note that this does not cover the 26.46 minutes it takes to walk 49 amos at the pace of an 18-minute mil.
The docs mention that Rabbi Yedidya Manet lists 3.65 degrees, which appears to be a bit too early.
user_docs.deprecated_note: Deprecated.

name: getTzaisGeonim3Point8Degrees
docs: This method returns the tzais (nightfall) based on the opinion of the Geonim calculated at the sun's position at {@link ZENITH_3_POINT_7 3.8°} below {@link GEOMETRIC_ZENITH geometric zenith} (90°), calculated as the position of the sun 13.5 minutes after sunset, the time it takes to walk 3/4 of a mil at 18 minutes a mil, plus 30 seconds for the time it takes to walk 49 amos (the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi). With this being on an 18-minutes mil, 49 amos would take 26.46, rounded to 30 seconds), for a total of 14 minutes after sunset. The sun is {@link ZENITH_3_POINT_8 3.8°} below {@link GEOMETRIC_ZENITH geometric zenith} at this time in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux. @return the Instant representing the time when the sun is 3.8° below sea level. @see ZENITH_3_POINT_8

user_docs.meaning: Tzais (nightfall) according to the Geonim, when the sun is 3.8 degrees below geometric zenith.
user_docs.calculation: Calculated as 3.8 degrees below geometric zenith, described as about 13.5 minutes after sunset. This is based on 3/4 of a mil at 18 minutes per mil, plus 30 seconds for the time to walk 49 amos, for a total of about 14 minutes after sunset.
notes: The docs note that this corresponds to the sun being 3.8 degrees below geometric zenith in Jerusalem around the equinox/equilux.
user_docs.deprecated_note: This zman is deprecated.

name: getTzaisGeonim5Point95Degrees
docs: This method returns the tzais (nightfall) based on the opinion of the Geonim calculated at the sun's position at {@link ZENITH_5_POINT_95 5.95°} below below {@link GEOMETRIC_ZENITH geometric zenith} (90°), calculated as the position of the sun 24 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux. The 24 minutes is based on the Baal Hatanya's calculation of 18 minutes (3/4 of a 24 minute mil) + 4 minutes for shkiah amitis + 2 minutes for bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi. See Hazmanim Bahalacha vol II, ch. 50, no. 5, p. 512-513, ch. 47, and Yisrael Vehazmanim Vol III, ch. 13, no. 53, p. 1026. Among sources he mentions for this zman is Rabbi Yehuda (Leo) Levi's calculations in Jewish Chrononomy and other sources. Calculations show that the time is closer to 5.93° and was seemingly rounded to 5.95°. Chabad calendars usually use the 6°-based {@link #getTzaisBaalHatanya()} that is built on this same calculation. It should be noted that Rabbi Yedidya Manet in his Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh (4th edition part 2, pages and 22 and 24) lists 5.88° that appears to be a drop too early. @return the Instant representing the time when the sun is 5.95° below sea level. If the calculation can't be computed such as northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see #getTzaisBaalHatanya()

user_docs.meaning: The time of tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, using the sun's position 5.95 degrees below geometric zenith.
user_docs.calculation: Calculated as the time when the sun is 5.95 degrees below sea level, corresponding to about 24 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox/equilux.
notes: This 24-minute basis comes from 18 minutes, plus 4 minutes for shkiah amitis, plus 2 minutes for bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi.
Sources cited in the documentation include Hazmanim Bahalacha vol II and Yisrael Vehazmanim vol III.
The documentation notes that the calculation appears closer to 5.93 degrees and was rounded to 5.95 degrees.
Chabad calendars usually use a 6-degree-based tzais built on the same calculation.

name: getTzaisGeonim4Point66Degrees
docs: This method returns the tzais (nightfall) based on the opinion of the Geonim calculated as 3/4 of a mil based on a 24-minute mil, or 18 minutes. It is the sun's position at {@link ZENITH_4_POINT_66 4.66°} below the western horizon. This is a very early zman and should not be relied on without Rabbinical guidance. This does not cover the 35.28 seconds it takes to walk 49 amos (the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi) at the pace of a 24-minute mil. See {@link #getTzaisGeonim4Point8Degrees()} for a time that covers the heref ayin. @return the Instant representing the time when the sun is 4.66° below sea level. If the calculation can't be computed such as northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see ZENITH_4_POINT_66 @see #getTzaisGeonim4Point8Degrees()

user_docs.meaning: The time of tzais (nightfall) according to the Geonim, based on when the sun is 4.66 degrees below the western horizon.
user_docs.calculation: Calculated as 3/4 of a mil based on a 24-minute mil, which is 18 minutes, and corresponds to the sun being 4.66 degrees below the western horizon.
notes: This is a very early zman and should not be relied on without Rabbinical guidance.
The zman may not be available in northern and southern locations where the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.
It does not cover the 35.28 seconds needed to walk 49 amos (the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi) at the pace of a 24-minute mil.
user_docs.deprecated_note: This zman is deprecated.

name: getTzaisGeonim4Point42Degrees
docs: This method returns the tzais (nightfall) based on the opinion of the Geonim calculated as 3/4 of a mil, based on a 22.5-minute mil, or 16 7/8 minutes. It is the sun's position at {@link ZENITH_4_POINT_42 4.42°} below the western horizon. This is a very early zman and should not be relied on without Rabbinical guidance. This does not cover the 33.07 seconds it takes to walk 49 amos (the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi) at the pace of a 22.5 minute-mil. It should be noted that Rabbi Yedidya Manet in his Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh (4th edition part 2, pages and 22 and 24) lists 4.37° that appears to be a drop too early. @return the Instant representing the time when the sun is 4.42° below sea level. If the calculation can't be computed such as northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see ZENITH_4_POINT_42

user_docs.meaning: Nightfall according to the Geonim, based on the sun being 4.42 degrees below the western horizon.
user_docs.calculation: Calculated as 3/4 of a mil, using a 22.5-minute mil, which is 16 7/8 minutes after sunset. It is based on the sun's position 4.42 degrees below sea level.
notes: This is a very early zman and should not be relied on without Rabbinical guidance.

The zman may not be available in northern and southern locations, including places south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, if the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon.

name: getTzaisGeonim4Point8Degrees
docs: This method returns the tzais (nightfall) based on the opinion of the Geonim calculated as the sun's position below the horizon at a time of 18.6 minutes after sunset. This is calculated as 3/4 of a 24-minute mil, plus 0.6 minutes for the time to walk 49 amos for bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi (with this zman based on a 24-minute mil, 49 amos would take 35.28 seconds to walk), for a total of 18.6 minutes after sunset. This calculates to the sun's position at {@link ZENITH_4_POINT_8 4.8°} below the western horizon. This is based on Rav Yechiel Michel Shlezinger's sefer Aizehu Bain Hashmashos, Rabbi Yehuda (Leo) Levi's calculations in Zmanei Hayom BaHalacha p. 37. At this point, 3 medium sized stars are visible to a non-expert with good vision with effort. An expert knowing where to look can see the 3 medium stars as early as 15 minutes after sunset. This is explained in detail in Hazmanim Bahalacha vol II, ch. 41, no. 6 (p. 372-373, ch. 47, no. 11-12 (p. 491-493) where it is clear that medium sized stars would be visible as early as 14 minutes after sunset (13.5 minutes for 3/4 of an 18 minute Mil, plus 0.5 minutes for bain Hashmashos of Rav Yosi) to an expert. See more details on this earier zman at {@link #getTzaisGeonim3Point8Degrees()}. This is an early zman for tzaisand should not be relied on without Rabbinical guidance. @return the Instant representing the time when the sun is 4.8° below sea level. If the calculation can't be computed such as northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see ZENITH_4_POINT_8

user_docs.meaning: This is an early tzais (nightfall) based on the Geonim, when the sun is 4.8 degrees below the western horizon. It is described as occurring about 18.6 minutes after sunset.
user_docs.calculation: It is calculated as 18.6 minutes after sunset, based on 3/4 of a 24-minute mil plus 0.6 minutes for the time to walk 49 amos during bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi.
notes: This is an early zman for tzais and should not be relied on without rabbinical guidance.
The zman may not be available in locations where the sun does not reach 4.8 degrees below the horizon, such as some northern and southern locations near the polar circles.

name: getTzaisGeonim6Point45Degrees
docs: This method returns the tzais (nightfall) based on the opinion of the Geonim as calculated by Rabbi Yechiel Michel Tucazinsky as the position of the sun no later than 31 minutes after sea-level sunset in Jerusalem (the Birur halacha shows that Rav Tucazinsky's calculations for sunset, listed as 28 minutes in this case, were about 3 minutes later than reality), and at the height of the summer solstice,this zman, calculatons show that 30.75 minutes after shkiah computes to 6.45° below {@link GEOMETRIC_ZENITH geometric zenith}. This calculation is found in the Birur Halacha Yoreh Deah 262 and it is the commonly used zman in Israel. It is also used in the Luach Itim Lebinah. it should be noted that this differs from the 6.1° / 6.2° calculation for Rabbi Tucazinsky's time as calculated by the Hazmanim Bahalacha Vol II chapter 50:7 (page 515). Calculations show that 6.45° at the equinox is 26.5 minutes after shkiah around the equinox / equilux. @return the Instant representing the time when the sun is 6.45° below sea level. If the calculation can't be computed such as northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see ZENITH_6_POINT_45

user_docs.meaning: Nightfall according to the Geonim, as calculated by Rabbi Yechiel Tucazinsky. It is the time when the sun is 6.45 degrees below sea level.
user_docs.calculation: Calculated as 6.45 degrees below geometric zenith, based on Rabbi Yechiel Tucazinsky's approach. The docs describe this as about 31 minutes after sea-level sunset in Jerusalem, and about 30.75 minutes after shkiah at the summer solstice.
notes: This is the commonly used zman in Israel.
It is also used in the [Luach Itim Lebinah](https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/243303103).
The Birur Halacha Yoreh Deah 262 discusses this calculation.
This differs from the 6.1 degree / 6.2 degree calculation associated with Rabbi Tucazinsky's time in Hazmanim Bahalacha Vol II chapter 50:7.
At the equinox, 6.45 degrees is about 26.5 minutes after shkiah.
The zman may not be available in northern and southern locations where the sun does not reach far enough below the horizon for this calculation.

name: getTzaisGeonim7Point083Degrees
docs: This method returns the tzais (nightfall) based on the opinion of the Geonim calculated when the sun's position {@link ZENITH_7_POINT_083 7.083° (or 7° 5′}) below the western horizon. This is often referred to as 7° 5′ or 7° and 5′ minutes. This calculation is based on the observation of 3 medium-sized stars by Dr. Baruch (Berthold) Cohn in his luach Tabellen enthaltend die Zeitangaben für den Beginn der Nacht und des Tages für die Breitengrade + 66 bis -38 published in Strasbourg, France in 1899. This calendar was very popular in Europe, and many other calendars based their tzais time on it. Rav Dovid Tzvi Hoffman in his שו״ת מלמד להועיל in an exchange of letters with Baruch Cohn in Orach Chaim 30 agreed to this zman (page 36), as did the שו״ת בני ציון and the תנובת שדה. It is very close to the time of the מקור חסד of the Sefer chasidim. It is close to the position of the sun 30 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux, but not Exactly. The actual position of the sun 30 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem at the equilux is 7.205° and 7.199° at the equinox. See Hazmanim Bahalacha vol 2, pages 520-521 for more details. @return the Instant representing the time when the sun is 7.083° below sea level. If the calculation can't be computed such as northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see ZENITH_7_POINT_083

user_docs.meaning: Nightfall according to the Geonim, when the sun is 7.083 degrees below the western horizon.
user_docs.calculation: Calculated for the sun being 7.083 degrees below sea level.
notes: This is often referred to as 7 degrees 5 minutes.
The calculation may not be available in far northern or far southern locations where the sun does not go low enough below the horizon.

name: getTzaisGeonim7Point67Degrees
docs: This method returns tzais (nightfall) based on the opinion of the Geonim calculated as 45 minutes after sunset during the summer solstice in New York, when the neshef (twilight) is the longest. The sun's position at this time computes to {@link ZENITH_7_POINT_67 7.75°} below the western horizon. See Igros Moshe Even Haezer 4, Ch. 4 (regarding tzais for krias Shema). It is also mentioned in Rabbi Heber's Shaarei Zmanim on in chapter 10 (page 87) and chapter 12 (page 108). Also see the time of 45 minutes in Rabbi Simcha Bunim Cohen's The radiance of Shabbos as the earliest zman for New York. This zman is also listed in the Divrei Shalom Vol. III, chapter 75, and Bais Av"i Vol. III, chapter 117. This zman is also listed in the Divrei Shalom etc. chapter 177. Since this zman depends on the level of light, Rabbi Yaakov Shakow presented this degree-based calculation to Rabbi Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky who agreed to it. @todo add hyperlinks to source of Divrei Shalom once it is located. @return the Instant representing the time when the sun is 7.67° below sea level. If the calculation can't be computed such as northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see ZENITH_7_POINT_67

user_docs.meaning: Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, using a 7.67 degrees-based definition.
user_docs.calculation: It is based on 45 minutes after sunset during the summer solstice in New York, when the twilight is longest. At that time, the sun is 7.67 degrees below the western horizon, or below sea level.
notes: This zman is tied to the Geonim's view of nightfall.
It is described as a degree-based alternative to a fixed 45-minute after sunset calculation.

name: getTzaisGeonim9Point3Degrees
docs: This method returns the tzais (nightfall) based on the calculations used in the Luach Itim Lebinah as the stringent time for tzais. It is calculated as the sun's position at {@link ZENITH_9_POINT_3 9.3°} below the western horizon. @return the Instant representing the time when the sun is 9.3° below sea level. If the calculation can't be computed such as northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation.

user_docs.meaning: The time of tzais (nightfall) based on the stringent calculation used in the [Luach Itim Lebinah](https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/243303103).
user_docs.calculation: When the sun is 9.3 degrees below the western horizon.
notes: This zman may not be available in northern and southern locations, including places south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

name: getTzaisGeonim9Point75Degrees
docs: This method returns the tzais (nightfall) based on the opinion of the Geonim calculated as 60 minutes after sunset around the equinox / equilux, the day that a solar hour is 60 minutes in New York. The sun's position at this time computes to {@link ZENITH_9_POINT_75 9.75°} below the western horizon. This is the opinion of Rabbi Eliyahu Henkin. This also follows the opinion of Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky. Rabbi Yaakov Shakow presented these degree-based times to Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky who agreed to them. @todo recalculate based on equinox / equilux. @return the Instant representing the time when the sun is 9.75° below sea level. If the calculation can't be computed such as northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see #getTzais60Minutes()

user_docs.meaning: Nightfall (tzais) based on the Geonim, when the sun is 9.75 degrees below the western horizon.
user_docs.calculation: Calculated for the point when the sun is 9.75 degrees below sea level. This is described as 60 minutes after sunset around the equinox/equilux, when a solar hour is 60 minutes in New York.
notes: This follows the view of Rabbi Eliyahu Henkin and also Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky.
The docs note that this degree-based time was presented to Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky by Rabbi Yaakov Shakow, and he agreed to it.

name: getTzais60Minutes

user_docs.meaning: Nightfall, based on the Chavas Yair and Divrei Malkiel view that a mil takes 15 minutes, so tzais is 60 minutes after sunset.
user_docs.calculation: 60 minutes after sea level sunset, based on 4 mil after sunset.
notes: {sea_level}
The calculation depends on the elevation setting.

name: getTzaisAteretTorah
docs: This method returns tzais usually calculated as 40 minutes (configurable to any offset via {@link #setAteretTorahSunsetOffset(double)}) after sunset. Please note that Chacham Yosef Harari-Raful of Yeshivat Ateret Torah who uses this time, does so only for calculating various other zmanei hayom such as Sof Zman Krias Shema and Plag Hamincha. His calendars do not publish a zman for Tzais. It should also be noted that Chacham Harari-Raful provided a 25 minute zman for Israel. This API uses 40 minutes year round in any place on the globe by default. This offset can be changed by calling {@link #setAteretTorahSunsetOffset(double)}. @return the Instant representing 40 minutes (configurable via {@link #setAteretTorahSunsetOffset}) after sea level sunset. If the calculation can't be computed such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year where the sun does not rise, and one where it does not set, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see #getAteretTorahSunsetOffset() @see #setAteretTorahSunsetOffset(double)

user_docs.meaning: The time of tzais usually used for Ateret Torah, calculated as 40 minutes after sunset by default.
user_docs.calculation: It is calculated as sea level sunset plus an offset that defaults to 40 minutes. The offset can be changed.
notes: This time is used by Chacham Yosef Harari-Raful of Yeshivat Ateret Torah for calculating other zmanim such as Sof Zman Krias Shema and Plag Hamincha, and his calendars do not publish a zman for tzais.
The default 40-minute offset is used year round anywhere on the globe unless changed.
The calculation may not be available where sunset cannot be calculated, such as in the Arctic Circle during periods when the sun does not rise or does not set.

name: getSofZmanShmaAteretTorah

user_docs.meaning: The latest time to recite morning Shema according to the Ateret Torah calculation.
user_docs.calculation: It is based on a day that starts 1/10 of the day before sunrise and usually ends {ateret_torah_offset} after sunset. The resulting temporal hours are measured across that day, and this zman is 3 temporal hours after alos 72 zmaniyos.
notes: Based on this calculation, chatzos will not be at midday.
The sunset ending time is usually 40 minutes after sunset, but it can be adjusted to a different offset.

name: getSofZmanTfilaAteretTorah

user_docs.meaning: The latest time to recite the morning prayers according to the Ateret Torah calculation.
user_docs.calculation: It is based on a day that starts 1/10 of the day before sunrise and usually ends {ateret_torah_offset} after sunset. The temporal hours for that day are measured from alos 72 zmaniyos, and this time is 4 shaos zmaniyos after alos 72 zmaniyos.
notes: Based on this calculation, chatzos is not at midday.
The zman may not be available if the calculation cannot be computed, such as in places and times where the sun does not rise or does not set.

name: getMinchaGedolaAteretTorah

user_docs.meaning: The earliest time for mincha gedola according to the Ateret Torah calculation, which is based on the view of Chacham Yosef Harari-Raful of Yeshivat Ateret Torah.
user_docs.calculation: It is 6.5 solar hours after alos, with alos defined as 1/10th of the day before sunrise and the day usually ending at {ateret_torah_offset} after sunset.
notes: This is presented as the preferred earliest time to pray mincha according to the opinion of the Rambam and others.

name: getMinchaKetanaAteretTorah

user_docs.meaning: The time for mincha ketana according to the Ateret Torah calculation. It is presented as the preferred earliest time to pray mincha according to the opinion of the Rambam and others.
user_docs.calculation: It is calculated as 9.5 solar hours after alos 72 zmanis. In this system, the day starts 1/10th of the day before sunrise and is usually treated as ending 40 minutes after sunset, with that sunset offset configurable.
notes: The sunset-based ending of the day is usually 40 minutes after sunset, but it can be set to a different offset.
If the calculation cannot be computed, the zman may not be available.

name: getPlagHaminchaAteretTorah

user_docs.meaning: The time of plag hamincha according to the Ateret Torah calculation.
user_docs.calculation: It is based on a day that begins 1/10 of the day before sunrise and usually ends 40 minutes after sunset, though the sunset offset can be adjusted to {ateret_torah_offset}. The zman is then calculated as 10.75 shaos zmaniyos after dawn.
notes: If the calculation cannot be computed, such as in places like the Arctic Circle where the sun may not rise or set on some days, the zman may not be available.

name: getTzais72Zmanis
docs: Method to return tzais (dusk) calculated as 72 minutes zmaniyos, or 1/10th of the day after {@link #getSeaLevelSunset() sea level sunset}. This is the way that the Minchas Cohen in Ma'amar 2:4 calculates Rebbeinu Tam's time of tzeis. It should be noted that this calculation results in the shortest time from sunset to tzais being during the winter solstice, the longest at the summer solstice and 72 clock minutes at the equinox. This does not match reality, since there is no direct relationship between the length of the day and twilight. The shortest twilight is during the equinox, the longest is during the summer solstice, and in the winter with the shortest daylight, the twilight period is longer than during the equinoxes. @return the Instant representing the time. If the calculation can't be computed such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year where the sun does not rise, and one where it does not set, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see #getAlos72Zmanis()

user_docs.meaning: Tzais, or dusk, calculated as 72 minutes zmaniyos after sea level sunset.
user_docs.calculation: This is computed as 1/10 of the day after sea level sunset.
notes: This is the way the Minchas Cohen in Ma'amar 2:4 calculates Rebbeinu Tam's time of tzeis.
This calculation results in the shortest time from sunset to tzais during the winter solstice, the longest at the summer solstice, and 72 clock minutes at the equinox.
This does not match reality, since there is no direct relationship between the length of the day and twilight.
The zman may not be available in locations such as the Arctic Circle, where there are days when the sun does not rise and days when it does not set.

name: getTzais90Zmanis

user_docs.meaning: Tzais, or dusk, based on 90 zmaniyos minutes - one eighth of the day - after sea level sunset. This is known in Yiddish as the achtel zman used in various kehilos.
user_docs.calculation: Add 90 zmaniyos minutes, or one eighth of the day, to sea level sunset.
notes: May not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in locations and dates where the sun does not rise or set.

name: getTzais96Zmanis

user_docs.meaning: Tzais (dusk) based on 96 minutes zmaniyos, which is 1/7.5 of the day after sea level sunset.
user_docs.calculation: This time is calculated as 96 minutes zmaniyos after sea level sunset.
notes: If the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set, the zman may not be available.

name: getTzais90Minutes

user_docs.meaning: The time of tzais, or nightfall, taken as 90 minutes after sunset. It is based on the Magen Avraham's opinion and the Rambam's view that walking a mil takes 18 minutes, for a total of 90 minutes after sunset.
user_docs.calculation: 90 minutes after sunset, using sea level sunset when elevation is not used and sunset when elevation is used.
notes: The time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle where the sun does not rise on some days and does not set on others.
A similar calculation uses solar position based on this time.

name: getTzais120Minutes
docs: This method should be used lechumra only and returns tzais (nightfall) based on the calculations of Rav Chaim Naeh that the time to walk the distance of a mil according to the Rambam's opinion is 2/5 of an hour (24 minutes) for a total of 120 minutes based on the opinion of Ula who calculated tzais as 5 mil after {@link #getSunset() sunset} or {@link #getSeaLevelSunset() sea level sunset} (depending on the {@link #isUseElevation()} setting). A similar calculation {@link #getTzais26Degrees()} uses degree-based calculations based on this 120 minute calculation. Since the zman is extremely late and at a point that is long past the 18° point where the darkest point is reached, it should only be used lechumra, such as delaying the start of nighttime mitzvos. @deprecated This method should be used lechumra only since it returns a very late time, and if used lekula can result in chillul Shabbos etc. There is no current plan to remove this method from the API, and this deprecation is intended to alert developers of the danger of using it. @return the Instant representing the time. If the calculation can't be computed such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year where the sun does not rise, and one where it does not set, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar}. documentation. @see #getTzais26Degrees() @see #getAlos120Minutes()

user_docs.meaning: A very late tzais (nightfall) based on a 120-minute delay after sunset, used only lechumra.
user_docs.calculation: It is calculated as 120 minutes after sunset, based on the opinion of Ula that tzais is 5 mil after sunset. The calculation uses sunset or sea level sunset depending on the elevation setting.
notes: This zman should be used only lechumra, such as delaying the start of nighttime mitzvos, because it is extremely late and well past the 18 degrees point where the darkest point is reached.
Based on the calculations of [Rav Chaim Naeh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Avraham_Chaim_Naeh) and the [Rambam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maimonides)'s opinion that a mil is 24 minutes.
If the calculation cannot be computed, the zman may not be available.
user_docs.deprecated_note: This zman is deprecated for regular use and should be used only lechumra. Using it lekula can result in chillul Shabbos and similar serious errors.

name: getTzais120Zmanis

user_docs.meaning: Dusk calculated as 120 zmaniyos minutes after sea level sunset. It is intended for lechumra use only, such as delaying the start of nighttime mitzvos.
user_docs.calculation: Calculated 120 zmaniyos minutes after sea level sunset.
notes: This zman is extremely late and is intended for lechumra only.
Since it is a very late time, using it lekula can result in chillul Shabbos and similar problems.
If the calculation cannot be computed, the zman may not be available.
user_docs.deprecated_note: Deprecated as a warning that it should be used lechumra only because it returns a very late time.

name: getTzais16Point1Degrees
docs: This calculates the time of tzais at the point when the sun is 16.1° below the horizon. This is the sun's dip below the horizon 72 minutes after sunset according Rabbeinu Tam's calculation of tzais around the equinox / equilux in Jerusalem. The question of equinox VS equilux is complex, with Rabbi Meir Posen in the Ohr Meir of the opinion that the equilux should be used. See Yisrael Vehazmanim vol I, 34:1:4. Rabbi Yedidya Manet in his Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh (4th edition part 2, pages and 22 and 24) and Rabbi Yonah Mertzbuch (in a letter published by Rabbi Manet) are of the opinion that the astronomical equinox should be used. The difference adds up to about 9 seconds, too trivial to make much of a difference. For information on how this is calculated see the comments on {@link #getAlos16Point1Degrees()}. @return the Instant representing the time. If the calculation can't be computed such as northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see #getTzais72Minutes() @see #getAlos16Point1Degrees() for more information on this calculation.

user_docs.meaning: The time of tzais when the sun is 16.1 degrees below the horizon.
user_docs.calculation: Calculated for the point when the sun reaches 16.1 degrees below the horizon. This is described as the sun's dip below the horizon 72 minutes after sunset according to Rabbeinu Tam's calculation of tzais around the equinox or equilux in Jerusalem.
notes: The equinox versus equilux question is complex. Rabbi Meir Posen, in the Ohr Meir, is cited as favoring the equilux, while Rabbi Yedidya Manet and Rabbi Yonah Mertzbuch are cited as favoring the astronomical equinox. The difference is about 9 seconds, which is usually too small to matter.
The time may not be available in locations where the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, such as places north of the Arctic Circle or south of the Antarctic Circle.
See the discussion of [equinox vs equilux zmanim calculations](https://kosherjava.com/2022/01/12/equinox-vs-equilux-zmanim-calculations/).

name: getTzais26Degrees
docs: This method should be used lechumra only and returns tzais based on when the sun is 26° below the horizon. For information on how this is calculated see the comments on {@link #getAlos26Degrees()}. Since the zman is extremely late and at a point when it is long past the 18° point where the darkest point is reached, it should only be used lechumra such as delaying the start of nighttime mitzvos. @deprecated This method should be used lechumra only since it returns a very late time, and if used lekula can result in chillul Shabbos etc. There is no current plan to remove this method from the API, and this deprecation is intended to alert developers of the danger of using it. @return the Instant representing the time. If the calculation can't be computed such as northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see #getTzais120Minutes() @see #getAlos26Degrees()

user_docs.meaning: Tzais based on when the sun is 26 degrees below the horizon.
user_docs.calculation: Calculated at the point when the sun is 26 degrees below the horizon.
notes: This zman should be used lechumra only.
Because it is extremely late and well past the 18-degree point where the darkest point is reached, it should only be used lechumra, such as delaying the start of nighttime mitzvos.
If the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, the zman may not be available.
user_docs.deprecated_note: This zman should be used lechumra only. Using it lekula can result in chillul Shabbos and similar problems. There is no current plan to remove it, and the warning is intended to alert developers to the danger of using it.

name: getTzais18Degrees

user_docs.meaning: The time of tzais at 18 degrees below the horizon, used as a nightfall calculation.
user_docs.calculation: Calculated using the same 18-degree-based approach referenced for alos 18 degrees, but for the evening time after sunset.
notes: If the sun does not reach 18 degrees below the horizon at a location, the zman may not be available or cannot be calculated.
See the detailed explanation in the AstronomicalCalendar documentation.

name: getTzais19Point8Degrees

user_docs.meaning: The time of tzais when the sun is 19.8 degrees below the horizon.
user_docs.calculation: Calculated the same way as alos 19.8 degrees, using the corresponding solar depression after sunset.
notes: This zman may not be available for northern and southern locations, including places south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach 19.8 degrees below the horizon.
See the detailed explanation on top of the AstronomicalCalendar documentation.

name: getTzais96Minutes

user_docs.meaning: The time of tzais (dusk) calculated as 96 minutes after sunset.
user_docs.calculation: It is based on either sunset or sea level sunset, depending on whether elevation is used. It is computed 96 minutes after that sunset time.
notes: {uses_elevation}
In places such as the Arctic Circle, this zman may not be available because there can be days when the sun does not rise or does not set.

name: getFixedLocalChatzosHayom
docs: A method that returns the local time for fixed chatzos. This time is noon and adjusted from standard time to account for the local latitude. The 360° of the globe divided by 24 calculates to 15° per hour with 4 minutes per degree, so at a longitude of 0 , 15, 30 etc... Chatzos is at exactly 12:00 noon. This is the time of chatzos according to the Aruch Hashulchan in Orach Chaim 233:14 and Rabbi Moshe Feinstein in Igros Moshe Orach Chaim 1:24 and 2:20. Lakewood, N.J., with a longitude of -74.222, is 0.778 away from the closest multiple of 15 at -75°. This is multiplied by 4 to yield 3 minutes and 7 seconds for a chatzos of 11:56:53. This method is not tied to the theoretical 15° time zones, but will adjust to the actual time zone and Daylight saving time. @return the Instant representing the local chatzos @see GeoLocation#getLocalMeanTimeOffset(Instant) @see #getLocalMeanTime(LocalTime)

user_docs.meaning: The local time of fixed chatzos, which is noon adjusted from standard time to reflect the local location. It represents chatzos according to the Aruch Hashulchan and Rabbi Moshe Feinstein.
user_docs.calculation: It starts with 12:00 noon and adjusts it by the location's difference from the nearest multiple of 15 degrees of longitude, using 4 minutes per degree. It also adjusts to the actual time zone and daylight saving time.
notes: The calculation is based on local longitude rather than the theoretical 15-degree time zones.
For example, Lakewood, N.J. at longitude -74.222 is 0.778 degrees from -75 degrees, which gives a fixed chatzos of 11:56:53.
[Aruch Hashulchan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aruch_HaShulchan)
[Rabbi Moshe Feinstein](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moshe_Feinstein)

name: getSofZmanKidushLevanaBetweenMoldos

user_docs.meaning: The latest time for Kiddush Levana according to the Maharil's opinion, calculated as halfway between one molad and the next. If this time falls during the day, alos is used instead.
user_docs.calculation: It adds half of the 29 days, 12 hours, and 793 chalakim between one molad and the next - that is, 14 days, 18 hours, 22 minutes, and 666 milliseconds - to the month's molad.
notes: The time is returned even if it occurs during the day.
To limit the time to between tzais and alos, use the version that accepts those boundaries.
If the zman will not occur on that day, the zman may not be available.

name: getSofZmanKidushLevana15Days
docs: Returns the latest time of Kiddush Levana calculated as 15 days after the molad. This is the opinion of the Shulchan Aruch (Orach Chaim 426). It should be noted that some opinions hold that the Rema who brings down the opinion of the Maharil's of calculating {@link #getSofZmanKidushLevanaBetweenMoldos(Instant, Instant) half way between molad and molad} is of the opinion that the Mechaber agrees to his opinion. Also see the Aruch Hashulchan. For additional details on the subject, See Rabbi Dovid Heber's very detailed write-up in Siman Daled (chapter 4) of Shaarei Zmanim. The sof zman Kiddush Levana will be returned even if it occurs during the day. To limit the time to between tzais and alos, see {@link #getSofZmanKidushLevana15Days(Instant, Instant)}. @return the Instant representing the moment 15 days after the molad. If the time occurs between alos and tzais, alos will be returned. If the zman will not occur on this day, a null will be returned. @see #getSofZmanKidushLevana15Days(Instant, Instant) @see #getSofZmanKidushLevanaBetweenMoldos() @see JewishCalendar#getSofZmanKidushLevana15Days()

user_docs.meaning: The latest time for Kiddush Levana, calculated as 15 days after the molad.
user_docs.calculation: It is based on 15 days after the molad, following the view of the Shulchan Aruch (Orach Chaim 426).
notes: Some opinions hold that the Rema, quoting the Maharil's approach of calculating half way between molad and molad, agrees with this view. See also the Aruch Hashulchan and Rabbi Dovid Heber's discussion in Shaarei Zmanim, Siman Daled (chapter 4).
The time is returned even if it occurs during the day.
To limit the time to between tzais and alos, use the version that takes those times into account.
The zman may not be available on a given day if it does not occur then.

name: getTchilasZmanKidushLevana3Days

user_docs.meaning: The earliest time for Kiddush Levana according to Rabbeinu Yonah's opinion, which allows it 3 days after the molad.
user_docs.calculation: It is the moment 3 days after the molad.
notes: This time is given even if it falls during the day, when Kiddush Levana cannot be said.
If the zman will not occur on that day, it may not be available.

name: getZmanMolad

user_docs.meaning: The point in time of the molad, or new moon, for that day.
notes: If the molad does not occur on this day, the zman may not be available.

name: getTchilasZmanKidushLevana7Days

user_docs.meaning: The earliest time for Kiddush Levana according to the views that it should not be said until 7 days after the molad.
user_docs.calculation: It is the moment 7 days after the molad, whether that falls during the day or at night.
notes: This time is returned even if it occurs during the day, when Kiddush Levana cannot be recited.
If you want to limit the time to night hours, use the version that accepts a time range.
If the zman does not occur on this day, it may not be available.

name: getSofZmanAchilasChametzGRA
docs: This method returns the latest time one is allowed eating chametz on Erev Pesach according to the opinion of the GRA. This time is identical to the {@link #getSofZmanTfilaGRA() Sof zman tfilah GRA} and is provided as a convenience method for those who are unaware how this zman is calculated. This time is 4 hours into the day based on the opinion of the GRA that the day is calculated from sunrise to sunset. This returns the time 4 * {@link #getShaahZmanisGRA()} after {@link #getSeaLevelSunrise() sea level sunrise}. If it is not erev Pesach, a null will be returned. @return the Instant one is allowed eating chametz on Erev Pesach. If it is not erev Pesach or the calculation can't be computed such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year where the sun does not rise, and one where it does not set, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see #getShaahZmanisGRA() @see #getSofZmanTfilaGRA() @see #getSofZmanAchilasChametz(Instant, Instant, boolean)

user_docs.meaning: The latest time chametz may be eaten on Erev Pesach according to the GRA. It is the same zman as Sof zman tfilah GRA.
user_docs.calculation: It is 4 seasonal hours after sea level sunrise, using the GRA's day from sunrise to sunset.
notes: {sea_level}
If it is not Erev Pesach, or if the zman cannot be calculated, the zman may not be available.

name: getSofZmanAchilasChametzMGA72Minutes

user_docs.meaning: The latest time chametz may be eaten on Erev Pesach according to the Magen Avraham opinion, using dawn 72 minutes before sunrise.
user_docs.calculation: This time is 4 shaos zmaniyos after dawn, where dawn is 72 minutes before sunrise.
notes: This time is identical to Sof zman tfilah MGA 72 minutes.
If it is not Erev Pesach, the zman may not be available.
If the calculation cannot be computed, such as in locations where the sun does not rise or does not set on a day, the zman may not be available.

name: getSofZmanAchilasChametzMGA72MinutesZmanis
docs: This method returns the latest time one is allowed eating chametz on Erev Pesach according to the opinion of the Magen Avraham (MGA) based on alos being {@link #getAlos72Zmanis() 72 zmaniyos} minutes before {@link #getSunset() sunrise}. This time is identical to the {@link #getSofZmanTfilaMGA72MinutesZmanis() Sof zman tfilah MGA 72 minutes zmanis}. This time is 4 {@link #getShaahZmanis72MinutesZmanis() shaos zmaniyos} (temporal hours) after {@link #getAlos72Minutes() dawn} based on the opinion of the MGA that the day is calculated from a {@link #getAlos72Zmanis() dawn} of 72 minutes zmanis before sunrise to {@link #getTzais72Zmanis() nightfall} of 72 minutes zmanis after sunset. This returns the time of 4 * {@link #getShaahZmanis72MinutesZmanis()} after {@link #getAlos72Zmanis() dawn}. If it is not erev Pesach, a null will be returned. @return the Instant of the latest time of eating chametz. If it is not erev Pesach or the calculation can't be computed such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year where the sun does not rise, and one where it does not set, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see #getShaahZmanis72MinutesZmanis() @see #getAlos72Zmanis() @see #getSofZmanTfilaMGA72MinutesZmanis() @see #getSofZmanAchilasChametz(Instant, Instant, boolean)

user_docs.meaning: The latest time one may eat chametz on Erev Pesach according to the Magen Avraham's view based on a day that begins 72 zmaniyos minutes before sunrise and ends 72 zmaniyos minutes after sunset.
user_docs.calculation: It is 4 shaos zmaniyos after dawn, using dawn defined as 72 zmaniyos minutes before sunrise. This time is the same as Sof zman tfilah according to the MGA 72 minutes zmanis calculation.
notes: If it is not Erev Pesach, the zman may not be available.
If the calculation cannot be computed, the zman may not be available.

name: getSofZmanAchilasChametzMGA16Point1Degrees

user_docs.meaning: The latest time one may eat chametz on Erev Pesach according to the Magen Avraham's opinion, using alos and nightfall at 16.1 degrees below the horizon.
user_docs.calculation: It is 4 shaos zmaniyos after alos 16.1 degrees before sunrise, with the day measured from dawn to nightfall using 16.1 degrees below the horizon for both ends.
notes: If it is not Erev Pesach, the zman may not be available.
The zman may not be available in locations and seasons where the sun does not reach the needed position below the horizon for this calculation.

name: getSofZmanBiurChametzGRA
docs: This method returns the latest time for burning chametz on Erev Pesach according to the opinion of the GRA. This time is 5 hours into the day based on the opinion of the GRA that the day is calculated from sunrise to sunset. This returns the time 5 * {@link #getShaahZmanisGRA()} after {@link #getSeaLevelSunrise() sea level sunrise}. If it is not erev Pesach, a null will be returned. @return the Instant of the latest time for burning chametz on Erev Pesach. If it is not erev Pesach or the calculation can't be computed such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year where the sun does not rise, and one where it does not set, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see #getShaahZmanisGRA() @see #getSofZmanBiurChametz(Instant, Instant, boolean)

user_docs.meaning: The latest time to burn chametz on Erev Pesach according to the GRA.
user_docs.calculation: It is 5 GRA seasonal hours after sea level sunrise, based on a day measured from sunrise to sunset.
notes: If it is not Erev Pesach, the zman may not be available.
The zman may not be available if it cannot be calculated, such as in places where the sun does not rise or does not set on that day.

name: getSofZmanBiurChametzMGA72Minutes

user_docs.meaning: The latest time to burn chametz on Erev Pesach according to the Magen Avraham opinion based on dawn being 72 minutes before sunrise.
user_docs.calculation: It is 5 shaos zmaniyos after dawn, where the day runs from dawn 72 minutes before sunrise to nightfall 72 minutes after sunset.
notes: If it is not Erev Pesach, the zman may not be available.
If the calculation cannot be computed, such as in locations where the sun does not rise or set on some days, the zman may not be available.

name: getSofZmanBiurChametzMGA72MinutesZmanis
docs: This method returns the latest time for burning chametz on Erev Pesach according to the opinion of the Magen Avraham (MGA) based on alos being {@link #getAlos72Zmanis() 72} minutes zmanis before {@link #getSunset() sunrise}. This time is 5 {@link #getShaahZmanis72MinutesZmanis() shaos zmaniyos} (temporal hours) after {@link #getAlos72Zmanis() dawn} based on the opinion of the MGA that the day is calculated from a {@link #getAlos72Zmanis() dawn} of 72 minutes zmanis before sunrise to {@link #getTzais72Zmanis() nightfall} of 72 minutes zmanis after sunset. This returns the time of 5 * {@link #getShaahZmanis72MinutesZmanis()} after {@link #getAlos72Zmanis() dawn}. If it is not erev Pesach, a null will be returned. @return the Instant of the latest time for burning chametz on Erev Pesach. If it is not erev Pesach or the calculation can't be computed such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year where the sun does not rise, and one where it does not set, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see #getShaahZmanis72MinutesZmanis() @see #getAlos72Zmanis() @see #getSofZmanBiurChametz(Instant, Instant, boolean)

user_docs.meaning: The latest time for burning chametz on Erev Pesach according to the Magen Avraham view, using a day that begins 72 minutes zmanis before sunrise and ends 72 minutes zmanis after sunset.
user_docs.calculation: It is calculated as 5 shaos zmaniyos after dawn, where dawn is 72 minutes zmanis before sunrise and each shaah zmanis is based on that same day length.
notes: If it is not Erev Pesach, the zman may not be available.
If the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there can be days with no sunrise or no sunset, the zman may not be available.

name: getSofZmanBiurChametzMGA16Point1Degrees
docs: This method returns the latest time for burning chametz on Erev Pesach according to the opinion of the Magen Avraham (MGA) based on alos being {@link #getAlos16Point1Degrees() 16.1°} before {@link #getSunset() sunrise}. This time is 5 {@link #getShaahZmanis16Point1Degrees() shaos zmaniyos} (solar hours) after {@link #getAlos16Point1Degrees() dawn} based on the opinion of the MGA that the day is calculated from dawn to nightfall with both being 16.1° below sunrise or sunset. This returns the time of 5 {@link #getShaahZmanis16Point1Degrees()} after {@link #getAlos16Point1Degrees() dawn}. If it is not erev Pesach, a null will be returned. @return the Instant of the latest time for burning chametz on Erev Pesach. If it is not erev Pesach or the calculation can't be computed such as northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see #getShaahZmanis16Point1Degrees() @see #getAlos16Point1Degrees() @see #getSofZmanBiurChametz(Instant, Instant, boolean)

user_docs.meaning: The latest time to burn chametz on Erev Pesach according to the Magen Avraham opinion, using a day that starts at dawn and ends at nightfall when the sun is 16.1 degrees below the horizon.
user_docs.calculation: It is 5 shaos zmaniyos after dawn, with dawn defined as 16.1 degrees before sunrise and the day measured from that dawn to the corresponding nightfall.
notes: If it is not Erev Pesach, the zman may not be available.
The zman may not be available if the calculation cannot be made, such as in far northern or southern locations where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon.

name: getAlosBaalHatanya
docs: Returns the Baal Hatanya's alos (dawn) calculated as the time when the sun is 16.9° below the eastern {@link GEOMETRIC_ZENITH geometric horizon} before {@link #getSunset() sunrise}. It is based on the calculation that the time between dawn and netz amiti (sunrise) is 72 minutes, the time that is takes to walk 4 mil at 18 minutes a mil (Rambam and others). The sun's position at 72 minutes before {@link #getSunriseBaalHatanya netz amiti (sunrise)} in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux is 16.9° below {@link GEOMETRIC_ZENITH geometric zenith}. @return The Instant of dawn. If the calculation can't be computed such as northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation.

user_docs.meaning: The Baal Hatanya's alos (dawn), defined as when the sun is 16.9 degrees below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise.
user_docs.calculation: It is based on the idea that the time from dawn to netz amiti (sunrise) is 72 minutes, using 18 minutes per mil for 4 mil. The 16.9 degrees figure is tied to the sun's position 72 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem around the equinox/equilux.
notes: If the sun does not get low enough below the horizon for this calculation, such as at some locations near or beyond the Arctic Circle or Antarctic Circle, the zman may not be available.

name: getSofZmanShmaBaalHatanya
docs: This method returns the latest zman krias shema (time to recite Shema in the morning). This time is 3 {@link #getShaahZmanisBaalHatanya() shaos zmaniyos} (solar hours) after {@link #getSunriseBaalHatanya() netz amiti (sunrise)} based on the opinion of the Baal Hatanya that the day is calculated from sunrise to sunset. This returns the time 3 * {@link #getShaahZmanisBaalHatanya()} after {@link #getSunriseBaalHatanya() netz amiti (sunrise)}. @see #getSofZmanShma(Instant, Instant) @see #getShaahZmanisBaalHatanya() @return the Instant of the latest zman shema according to the Baal Hatanya. If the calculation can't be computed such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year where the sun does not rise, and one where it does not set, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation.

user_docs.meaning: The latest time to recite Shema in the morning according to the Baal Hatanya.
user_docs.calculation: It is 3 shaos zmaniyos after sunrise (netz amiti), using the Baal Hatanya's view that the day is measured from sunrise to sunset.
notes: If sunrise or sunset cannot be determined, this zman may not be available.

name: getSofZmanTfilaBaalHatanya

user_docs.meaning: The latest time for morning prayer according to the Baal Hatanya, based on a day measured from sunrise to sunset.
user_docs.calculation: This is 4 shaah zmanis after sunrise, using the Baal Hatanya calculation of daylight from sunrise to sunset.
notes: If sunrise or sunset cannot be calculated, this zman may not be available.
This is also called zman tfilah.

name: getSofZmanAchilasChametzBaalHatanya

user_docs.meaning: The latest time chametz may be eaten on Erev Pesach according to the Baal Hatanya.
user_docs.calculation: This is the same time as the Baal Hatanya sof zman tfila: 4 halachic hours into the day, counted from sunrise to sunset. It is 4 shaos zmaniyos after Baal Hatanya sunrise (netz amiti).
notes: If it is not Erev Pesach, this zman may not be available.
If the time cannot be calculated, such as in locations with extreme day or night conditions, this zman may not be available.

name: getSofZmanBiurChametzBaalHatanya
docs: This method returns the latest time for burning chametz on Erev Pesach according to the opinion of the Baal Hatanya. This time is 5 hours into the day based on the opinion of the Baal Hatanya that the day is calculated from sunrise to sunset. This returns the time 5 * {@link #getShaahZmanisBaalHatanya()} after {@link #getSunriseBaalHatanya() netz amiti (sunrise)}. If it is not erev Pesach, a null will be returned. @return the Instant of the latest time for burning chametz on Erev Pesach. If it is not erev Pesach or the calculation can't be computed such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year where the sun does not rise, and one where it does not set, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see #getShaahZmanisBaalHatanya() @see #getSofZmanBiurChametz(Instant, Instant, boolean)

user_docs.meaning: The latest time to burn chametz on Erev Pesach according to the Baal Hatanya.
user_docs.calculation: It is 5 shaos zmaniyos after netz amiti (sunrise), using the Baal Hatanya's day length from sunrise to sunset.
notes: If it is not Erev Pesach, the zman may not be available.

name: getMinchaGedolaBaalHatanya
docs: This method returns the time of mincha gedola. Mincha gedola is the earliest time one can pray mincha. The Rambam is of the opinion that it is better to delay mincha until {@link #getMinchaKetanaBaalHatanya() mincha ketana} while the Ra"sh, Tur, GRA and others are of the opinion that mincha can be prayed lechatchila starting at mincha gedola. This is calculated as 6.5 {@link #getShaahZmanisBaalHatanya() sea level solar hours} after {@link #getSunriseBaalHatanya() netz amiti (sunrise)}. This calculation is based on the opinion of the Baal Hatanya that the day is calculated from sunrise to sunset. This returns the time 6.5 * {@link #getShaahZmanisBaalHatanya()} after {@link #getSunriseBaalHatanya() netz amiti ("real" sunrise)}. @todo Consider adjusting this to calculate the time as 30 clock or zmaniyos minutes after either {@link getSunTransit() astronomical chatzos} or {@link #getChatzosHayomAsHalfDay() chatzos as half a day} for {@link AstronomicalCalculator calculators} that support it, based on {@link #isUseAstronomicalChatzos()}. @see #getMinchaGedola(Instant, Instant) @see #getShaahZmanisBaalHatanya() @see #getMinchaKetanaBaalHatanya() @return the Instant of the time of mincha gedola according to the Baal Hatanya. If the calculation can't be computed such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year where the sun does not rise, and one where it does not set, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation.

user_docs.meaning: The time of mincha gedola, the earliest time one can pray mincha according to the Baal Hatanya calculation.
user_docs.calculation: It is calculated as 6.5 sea level solar hours after sunrise, using the Baal Hatanya view that the day runs from sunrise to sunset.
notes: This is 6.5 seasonal hours after netz amiti, meaning real sunrise.
If the calculation cannot be computed, such as in places where the sun does not rise or does not set on at least one day of the year, the zman may not be available.

name: getMinchaKetanaBaalHatanya
docs: This method returns the time of mincha ketana. This is the preferred earliest time to pray mincha in the opinion of the Rambam and others. For more information on this see the documentation on {@link #getMinchaGedolaBaalHatanya() mincha gedola}. This is calculated as 9.5 {@link #getShaahZmanisBaalHatanya() sea level solar hours} after {@link #getSunriseBaalHatanya() netz amiti (sunrise)}. This calculation is calculated based on the opinion of the Baal Hatanya that the day is calculated from sunrise to sunset. This returns the time 9.5 * {@link #getShaahZmanisBaalHatanya()} after {@link #getSunriseBaalHatanya() netz amiti (sunrise)}. @see #getMinchaKetana(Instant, Instant) @see #getShaahZmanisBaalHatanya() @see #getMinchaGedolaBaalHatanya() @return the Instant of the time of mincha ketana. If the calculation can't be computed such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year where the sun does not rise, and one where it does not set, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation.

user_docs.meaning: The preferred earliest time to pray mincha in the opinion of the Rambam and others, according to the Baal Hatanya calculation.
user_docs.calculation: It is calculated as 9.5 sea level solar hours after netz amiti (sunrise), using the Baal Hatanya view that the day is measured from sunrise to sunset.
notes: For more information, see the discussion of mincha gedola.
The time may not be available if the calculation cannot be computed, such as in places and times where the sun does not rise or does not set.

name: getPlagHaminchaBaalHatanya
docs: This method returns the time of plag hamincha. This is calculated as 10.75 hours after sunrise. This calculation is based on the opinion of the Baal Hatanya that the day is calculated from sunrise to sunset. This returns the time 10.75 * {@link #getShaahZmanisBaalHatanya()} after {@link #getSunriseBaalHatanya() netz amiti (sunrise)}. See About Our Zmanim Calculations @ Chabad.org for more details on this calculation. @see #getPlagHamincha(Instant, Instant) @return the Instant of the time of plag hamincha. If the calculation can't be computed such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year where the sun does not rise, and one where it does not set, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation.

user_docs.meaning: The time of plag hamincha according to the Baal Hatanya calculation.
user_docs.calculation: Calculated as 10.75 hours after sunrise, based on the view that the day is measured from sunrise to sunset.
notes: This is 10.75 times the Baal Hatanya proportional hour after netz amiti (sunrise).
The zman may not be available or cannot be calculated in places and seasons where the sun does not rise or does not set.
See [About Our Zmanim Calculations](https://www.chabad.org/library/article_cdo/aid/3209349/jewish/About-Our-Zmanim-Calculations.htm) for more details.

name: getTzaisBaalHatanya
docs: A method that returns tzais (nightfall) when the sun is 6° below the western geometric horizon (90°) after {@link #getSunset() sunset}. This tzais / nightfall based on the opinion of the Baal Hatanya. This calculation is based on the position of the sun about 24 minutes after {@link #getSeaLevelSunset() sunset} in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux, which is 6° below {@link GEOMETRIC_ZENITH geometric zenith}. See About Our Zmanim Calculations @ Chabad.org that is based on {@link #getSunsetBaalHatanya() shkiah amitis as 1.583° below the horizon} calculated around the equinox / equilux that computes 3.516 minutes after sunset. To this, 18 minutes of 3/4 of a 24-minute mil and two minutes for bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi is added. This calculation computes the the sun being 5.83° below the horizon (very close to the slightly later {@link #getTzaisGeonim5Point95Degrees()} that was calculated based on 4 fixed minutes) and it is rounded up to 6°. @return The Instant of nightfall. If the calculation can't be computed such as northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see #getTzaisGeonim5Point95Degrees() @see #getSunsetBaalHatanya()

user_docs.meaning: Nightfall according to the Baal Hatanya.
user_docs.calculation: This is when the sun is 6 degrees below the western geometric horizon after sunset. The calculation is based on the Baal Hatanya's view of nightfall, using a model tied to sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox/equilux.
notes: The calculation is described as using about 24 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox/equilux, including 18 minutes plus 2 minutes for bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi.
It is based on shkiah amitis as 1.583 degrees below the horizon.

At some northern and southern locations, including places even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the zman may not be available if the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon.

name: getSofZmanShmaMGA18DegreesToFixedLocalChatzos

user_docs.meaning: The latest time to recite morning Shema according to the Magen Avraham view, using Rav Moshe Feinstein's approach of measuring the day from dawn to nightfall but using only the first half of the day.
user_docs.calculation: It is based on a half-day that begins at alos defined as 18 degrees and ends at fixed local chatzos. Sof zman Shema is 3 shaos zmaniyos after alos, or half of that half-day.
notes: In locations where the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, the zman may not be available.

name: getSofZmanShmaMGA16Point1DegreesToFixedLocalChatzos

user_docs.meaning: The latest time to recite Shema in the morning according to the Magen Avraham approach, using the first half of the day. Here, the day begins at alos set to 16.1 degrees and ends at fixed local chatzos.
user_docs.calculation: It is 3 shaos zmaniyos after alos at 16.1 degrees, with the half-day measured from that alos until fixed local chatzos.
notes: This follows Rav Moshe Feinstein's opinion.
The zman may not be available in locations where the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

name: getSofZmanShmaMGA90MinutesToFixedLocalChatzos
docs: This method returns Rav Moshe Feinstein's opinion of the calculation of sof zman krias shema (latest time to recite Shema in the morning) according to the opinion of the Magen Avraham (MGA) that the day is calculated from dawn to nightfall, but calculated using the first half of the day only. The half a day starts at alos defined as {@link #getAlos90Minutes() 90 minutes before sunrise} and ends at {@link #getFixedLocalChatzosHayom() fixed local chatzos}. Sof Zman Shema is 3 shaos zmaniyos (solar hours) after this alos or half of this half-day. @return the Instant of the latest zman krias shema. If the calculation can't be computed such as northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see #getAlos90Minutes() @see #getFixedLocalChatzosHayom() @see #getHalfDayBasedZman(Instant, Instant, double)

user_docs.meaning: The latest time to recite Shema in the morning according to Rav Moshe Feinstein's approach to the Magen Avraham opinion, using a day measured from dawn to fixed local chatzos.
user_docs.calculation: It is calculated as 3 shaos zmaniyos after alos, where alos is 90 minutes before sunrise, and the half-day ends at fixed local chatzos. In other words, it is half of the half-day from 90 minutes before sunrise to fixed local chatzos.
notes: The zman may not be available in locations, including some places north or south of the Arctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

name: getSofZmanShmaMGA72MinutesToFixedLocalChatzos

The half a day starts at alos defined as {@link #getAlos72Minutes() 72 minutes before sunrise} and ends at {@link #getFixedLocalChatzosHayom() fixed local chatzos}. Sof Zman Shema is 3 shaos zmaniyos (solar hours) after this alos or half of this half-day. @return the Instant of the latest zman krias shema. user_docs.meaning: The latest time to recite Shema in the morning according to the Magen Avraham opinion, using a day measured from dawn to nightfall but based on the first half of the day only.
user_docs.calculation: The half-day starts at alos defined as 72 minutes before sunrise and ends at fixed local chatzos. Sof zman Shema is 3 shaos zmaniyos after this alos, which is half of that half-day.

Uses alos defined as 72 minutes before sunrise and fixed local chatzos.

name: getSofZmanShmaGRASunriseToFixedLocalChatzos

user_docs.meaning: The latest time to recite the morning Shema according to Rav Moshe Feinstein's view of the GRA calculation, using only the first half of the day from sunrise to fixed local chatzos.
user_docs.calculation: It is calculated as 3 shaos zmaniyos after sunrise, based on the half-day from sunrise until fixed local chatzos. In other words, it is the midpoint of that half-day.
notes: If the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, the zman may not be available.

name: getSofZmanTfilaGRASunriseToFixedLocalChatzos

user_docs.meaning: The latest time to recite the morning prayers according to Rav Moshe Feinstein's opinion, following the GRA's day definition but based on the first half of the day.
user_docs.calculation: It is calculated from sunrise to fixed local chatzos. The result is 4 shaos zmaniyos after sunrise, which is also 2/3 of that first half of the day.
notes: The half-day starts at sunrise and ends at fixed local chatzos.
In locations where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, the zman may not be available.

name: getMinchaGedolaGRAFixedLocalChatzos30Minutes

user_docs.calculation: It is calculated as 30 minutes after fixed local chatzos.
notes: If the zman cannot be calculated, such as in places like the Arctic Circle where the sun does not rise on at least one day of the year and does not set on another, the zman may not be available.

name: getMinchaKetanaGRAFixedLocalChatzosToSunset

user_docs.meaning: The time of mincha ketana according to Rav Moshe Feinstein's opinion, using the GRA-based calculation of 3.5 shaos zmaniyos after fixed local chatzos.
user_docs.calculation: Calculated as 3.5 shaos zmaniyos after fixed local chatzos, using the GRA method.
notes: This is the preferred time to recite the mincha prayers according to the opinion of the Rambam and others.

If the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year with no sunrise and one with no sunset, the zman may not be available or cannot be calculated in that situation.

name: getPlagHaminchaGRAFixedLocalChatzosToSunset

user_docs.meaning: The time of plag hamincha calculated using the GRA approach, with the day ending at sunset and fixed local chatzos as the midpoint.
user_docs.calculation: It is 4.75 shaos zmaniyos after fixed local chatzos, based on a day that runs from fixed local chatzos to sunset.
notes: This reflects Rav Moshe Feinstein's opinion of plag hamincha.
The zman may not be available in locations or seasons where the calculation cannot be made, such as places with days when the sun does not rise or does not set.

name: getTzais50Minutes
docs: Method to return tzais (dusk) calculated as 50 minutes after {@link #getSunset() sunset} or {@link #getSeaLevelSunset() sea level sunset} (depending on the {@link #isUseElevation()} setting). This method returns tzais (nightfall) based on the opinion of Rabbi Moshe Feinstein for the New York area. This time should not be used for latitudes other than ones similar to the latitude of the NY area. @return the Instant representing the time. If the calculation can't be computed such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year where the sun does not rise, and one where it does not set, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation.

user_docs.meaning: The time of tzais, or nightfall, set as 50 minutes after sunset.
user_docs.calculation: It is calculated as 50 minutes after sunset, or after sea level sunset depending on the elevation setting.
notes: {uses_elevation}
This is based on the opinion of Rabbi Moshe Feinstein for the New York area.
It should not be used for locations at latitudes unlike the New York area.
The zman may not be available if it cannot be calculated.

name: getSamuchLeMinchaKetanaGRA
docs: A method for calculating samuch lemincha ketana, / near mincha ketana time that is half an hour before {@link #getMinchaKetanaGRA()} or is 9 * shaos zmaniyos (solar hours) after the start of the day, calculated according to the GRA using a day starting at sunrise and ending at sunset. This is the time that eating or other activity can't begin prior to praying mincha. The calculation used is 9 * {@link #getShaahZmanisGRA()} after {@link #getSunset() sunrise} or {@link #getSunriseBasedOnElevationSetting() elevation adjusted sunrise} (depending on the {@link #isUseElevation()} setting). See the Mechaber and Mishna Berurah 232 and 249:2. @see #getShaahZmanisGRA() @see #getSamuchLeMinchaKetana(Instant, Instant, boolean) @see #isUseAstronomicalChatzosForOtherZmanim() @return the Instant of the time of samuch lemincha ketana. If the calculation can't be computed such as northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation.

user_docs.meaning: The time known as samuch lemincha ketana, or near mincha ketana. It is a point when eating or other activity should not begin before praying mincha.
user_docs.calculation: It is calculated as 9 shaos zmaniyos after the start of the day, using the GRA day from sunrise to sunset. It is also described as half an hour before mincha ketana. The calculation uses sunrise, or elevation-adjusted sunrise when elevation is used.
notes: {uses_elevation}

The zman may not be available for some extreme northern or southern locations, including places south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

name: getSamuchLeMinchaKetana16Point1Degrees

name: getSamuchLeMinchaKetana72Minutes

user_docs.meaning: The time known as samuch lemincha ketana - the point about half an hour before mincha ketana, when eating or other activity should not begin before praying mincha.
