name: getSunsetOrWesternmostSolarAzimuth
docs: {@summary A method that returns {@link #getSunsetBasedOnElevationSetting() sunset} if it occurs, or the time that the sun is at its westernmost position (azimuth of 270°), if sunset will not occur that day. In Polar regions (the Arctic or Antarctic circles), there are days of no sunrise or sunset, and there are opinions that during these periods, the day-night boundary is when the sun is at its westernmost position}. Sunrise in this opinion is when the sun is at {@link #getSunriseOrEasternmostSolarAzimuth() azimuth 90°, its easternmost position}. This is the opinion of Rabbi Yehosef Schwarz in his דברי יוסף – דרך מבוא השמש and דברי יוסף – תשובות, שאלה ח׳. This is brought down lehalacha by The Ben Ish Chai in the רב פעלים – חלק ב׳, סוד ישרים ס׳ ד׳. This time is close to six hours after {@link #getSunTransit() astronomical chatzos hayom}, but depending on the time of year and location in the Arctic / Antarctic, it can be up to 46 minutes before or after this time. @return sunset if it occurs, or the time that the sun will reach its westernmost position (azimuth 270°), if sunset will not occur that day. If there is no sunset this day, and the azimuth 270° will not occur, a null will be returned. @see #getSunriseOrEasternmostSolarAzimuth() @see #getTimeAtAzimuth(double) @see com.kosherjava.zmanim.util.AstronomicalCalculator#getTimeAtAzimuth(LocalDate, GeoLocation, double)

user_docs.meaning: The time of sunset if sunset occurs. If sunset does not occur that day, it is the time when the sun reaches its westernmost position, at an azimuth of 270 degrees.
user_docs.calculation: Use sunset based on the elevation setting when sunset occurs. If there is no sunset that day, use the time when the sun is at its westernmost azimuth of 270 degrees.
notes: In polar regions, where there are days without sunrise or sunset, this is used as the day-night boundary in the opinion cited in the docs.
If there is no sunset and the sun never reaches an azimuth of 270 degrees, the zman may not be available.

name: getSunriseOrEasternmostSolarAzimuth
docs: {@summary A method that returns {@link #getSunriseBasedOnElevationSetting() sunrise} if it occurs, or the time that the sun is at its easternmost position (azimuth of 90°), if sunrise will not occur that day. In Polar regions (the Arctic or Antarctic circles), there are days of no sunrise or sunset, and there are opinions that during these periods, the day-night boundary is when the sun is at its westernmost position}. Sunset in this opinion is when the sun is at {@link #getSunsetOrWesternmostSolarAzimuth() azimuth 270°, its westernmost position}. This is the opinion of Rabbi Yehosef Schwarz in his דברי יוסף – דרך מבוא השמש and דברי יוסף – תשובות, שאלה ח׳. This is brought down lehalacha by The Ben Ish Chai in the רב פעלים – חלק ב׳, סוד ישרים ס׳ ד׳. This time is close to six hours before {@link #getSunTransit() astronomical chatzos hayom}, but depending on the time of year and location in the Arctic / Antarctic, it can be up to 46 minutes before or after this time. @return sunrise if it occurs, or the time that the sun will reach its easternmost position (azimuth 90°), if sunrise will not occur that day. If there is no sunrise this day, and the azimuth 90° will not occur, a null will be returned. @see #getSunsetOrWesternmostSolarAzimuth() @see #getTimeAtAzimuth(double) @see com.kosherjava.zmanim.util.AstronomicalCalculator#getTimeAtAzimuth(LocalDate, GeoLocation, double)

user_docs.meaning: The time used for sunrise is the regular sunrise if sunrise occurs. If there is no sunrise that day, it is the moment when the sun reaches its easternmost position, at an azimuth of 90 degrees.
user_docs.calculation: Uses sunrise when it occurs. If sunrise does not occur, it uses the time when the sun is at azimuth 90 degrees. In polar regions, this is part of the view that the day-night boundary is marked by the sun's easternmost position when sunrise and sunset do not occur.
notes: In polar regions, there are days with no sunrise or sunset.
This time is close to six hours before astronomical chatzos hayom, but it can be up to 46 minutes before or after that depending on the season and location.
If there is no sunrise that day and the sun never reaches azimuth 90 degrees, the zman may not be available.

name: getTzaisGeonim8Point5Degrees
docs: A method that returns tzais (nightfall) when the sun is {@link ZENITH_8_POINT_5 8.5°} below the {@link GEOMETRIC_ZENITH geometric horizon} (90°) after {@link getSunset() sunset}, a time that Rabbi Meir Posen in his the Ohr Meir calculated that 3 small stars are visible, which is later than the required 3 medium stars. This calculation is based on the sun's position below the horizon 36 minutes after {@link getSeaLevelSunrise() sunset} in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux, which is 8.5° below {@link GEOMETRIC_ZENITH geometric zenith}. @return The Instant of nightfall. If the calculation can't be computed such as northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar#getTzaisGeonim8Point5Degrees() that returns an identical time to this generic tzais

user_docs.meaning: Nightfall when the sun is 8.5 degrees below the geometric horizon after sunset. It is associated with the time Rabbi Meir Posen calculated that 3 small stars are visible, which is later than the required 3 medium stars.
user_docs.calculation: Based on the sun being 8.5 degrees below the geometric zenith, described as about 36 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox/equilux.
notes: If the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, the zman may not be available. This can happen in some northern and southern locations, including places south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle.

name: getAlos16Point1Degrees
docs: Returns alos (dawn) based on the time when the sun is {@link ZENITH_16_POINT_1 16.1°} below the eastern {@link GEOMETRIC_ZENITH geometric horizon} before {@link getSunrise() sunrise}. This is based on the calculation that the time between dawn and sunrise (and sunset to nightfall) is 72 minutes, the time that is takes to walk 4 mil at 18 minutes a mil (Rambam and others). The sun's position below the horizon 72 minutes before {@link getSunrise() sunrise} in Jerusalem on the around the equinox / equilux is 16.1° below {@link GEOMETRIC_ZENITH}. @see ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar#getTzais16Point1Degrees() @return The Instant of dawn. If the calculation can't be computed such as northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation.

user_docs.meaning: The dawn time when the sun is 16.1 degrees below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise.
user_docs.calculation: It is based on the idea that the time from dawn to sunrise is 72 minutes, using 4 mil at 18 minutes per mil. The 16.1-degree depression is the sun's position 72 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem around the equinox.
notes: This follows the 72-minute dawn-to-sunrise convention associated with 4 mil at 18 minutes per mil.
The time may not be available for locations where the sun does not reach 16.1 degrees below the horizon.

name: getAlos72Minutes
docs: Method to return alos (dawn) calculated as 72 minutes before {@link getSunrise() sunrise} or {@link getSeaLevelSunrise() sea level sunrise} (depending on the {@link isUseElevation()} setting). This time is based on the time to walk the distance of 4 mil at 18 minutes a mil. The 72-minute time (but not the concept of fixed minutes) is based on the opinion that the time of the Neshef (twilight between dawn and sunrise) does not vary by the time of year or location but depends on the time it takes to walk the distance of 4 mil. @return the Instant representing the time. If the calculation can't be computed such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year where the sun does not rise, and one where it does not set, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation.

user_docs.meaning: Alos, or dawn, calculated as 72 minutes before sunrise. If elevation is being used, it is 72 minutes before sea level sunrise instead.
user_docs.calculation: Computed as 72 minutes before sunrise, based on 18 minutes per mil for 4 mil.
notes: {uses_elevation}
This zman may not be available if it cannot be calculated, such as in locations where the sun does not rise on at least one day of the year and does not set on another.

name: getChatzosHayom
docs: This method returns {@link getSunTransit() Astronomical chatzos hayom} if the {@link com.kosherjava.zmanim.util.AstronomicalCalculator calculator} class used supports it and {@link isUseAstronomicalChatzos() isUseAstronomicalChatzos()} is set to true or the {@link getChatzosHayomAsHalfDay() halfway point between sunrise and sunset} if it does not support it, or it is not configured to use it. There are currently two {@link com.kosherjava.zmanim.util.AstronomicalCalculator calculators} available in the API, the default {@link com.kosherjava.zmanim.util.NOAACalculator NOAA calculator} and the {@link com.kosherjava.zmanim.util.SunTimesCalculator USNO calculator}. The USNO calculator calculates chatzos as halfway between sunrise and sunset (identical to six shaos zmaniyos after sunrise), while the NOAACalculator calculates it more accurately as {@link getSunTransit() astronomical chatzos}. See The Definition of Chatzos for a detailed explanation of the ways to calculate Chatzos. Since half-day chatzos can be null in the Arctic on a day when either sunrise or sunset did not happen and astronomical chatzos can be calculated even in the Arctic, if half-day chatzos calculates as null and astronomical chatzos is supported by the calculator, astronomical chatzos will be returned to avoid returning a null. @see getSunTransit() @see getChatzosHayomAsHalfDay() @see isUseAstronomicalChatzos() @see setUseAstronomicalChatzos(boolean) @return the Instant of chatzos. If the calculation can't be computed such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day where the sun does not rise, and one where it does not set, and the calculator does not support astronomical calculations (that will never report a null) a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation.

user_docs.meaning: The day's chatzos hayom, meaning either astronomical noon or, if astronomical chatzos is not being used, the halfway point between sunrise and sunset.
user_docs.calculation: When astronomical chatzos is enabled, it uses sun transit (astronomical chatzos). Otherwise, it uses the halfway point between sunrise and sunset.
notes: With NOAA, astronomical chatzos is supported.

Halfway-between-sunrise-and-sunset chatzos may not be available in the Arctic on days when sunrise or sunset does not occur; when astronomical chatzos is supported, that value is used to avoid an unavailable result.

name: getChatzosHalayla
docs: A method that returns chatzos halayla at the end of the day (the last zman of the day returned by the calendar, that may actually be after midnight of the day it is being calculated for). For example, if calculating it for the date of Erev Pesach, the calculation will be for Lail Pesach to allow you to use the zman as sof zman achilas chametz. {@link getSolarMidnight() Astronomical chatzos halayla} will be returned if the {@link com.kosherjava.zmanim.util.AstronomicalCalculator calculator} class used supports it and {@link isUseAstronomicalChatzos() isUseAstronomicalChatzos()} is set to true. Otherwise the {@link #getChatzos(Instant, Instant) halfway point} between sunset and the following day's sunrise, if it does not support it, or it is not configured to use it. There are currently two {@link com.kosherjava.zmanim.util.AstronomicalCalculator calculators} available in the API, the default {@link com.kosherjava.zmanim.util.NOAACalculator NOAA calculator} and the {@link com.kosherjava.zmanim.util.SunTimesCalculator USNO calculator}. The USNO calculator calculates chatzos halayla as halfway between sunset and the following day's sunrise (identical to six shaos zmaniyos after sunset), while the NOAACalculator calculates it more accurately as {@link getSolarMidnight() astronomical chatzos halayla}. See The Definition of Chatzos for a detailed explanation of the ways to calculate Chatzos. Since half-night chatzos can be null in the Arctic on a day when either sunset or the following day's sunrise did not happen and astronomical chatzos halayla can be calculated even in the Arctic, if half-day chatzos calculates as null and astronomical chatzos is supported by the calculator, astronomical chatzos will be returned to avoid returning a null. @see getSolarMidnight() @see getChatzos(Instant, Instant) @see isUseAstronomicalChatzos() @see setUseAstronomicalChatzos(boolean) @return the Instant of chatzos halayla at the end of the current day. If the calculation can't be computed such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day where the sun does not rise, and one where it does not set, and the calculator does not support astronomical calculations (that will never report a null) a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation.

user_docs.meaning: The halachic midnight at the end of the day - the last zman of the day, which may actually be after midnight on the civil clock. It is used, for example, on Erev Pesach so it can serve as sof zman achilas chametz for the following night.
user_docs.calculation: With NOAA, it is calculated as astronomical chatzos halayla, meaning solar midnight at the end of the day.
notes: If astronomical chatzos is enabled, the result is solar midnight rather than halfway between sunset and the following day's sunrise.

name: getChatzosHayomAsHalfDay

user_docs.meaning: Chatzos ha-yom calculated as the midpoint between sunrise and sunset.
user_docs.calculation: It is calculated as halfway between sunrise and sunset. The docs also say that, when used with NOAA-based calculations, it can be treated as six shaos zmaniyos after sunrise.
notes: Some opinions define chatzos this way, even though it is not the most accurate calculation.
Because day length changes, this is not always the exact midpoint of the day, but it is very close.
If the calculation cannot be computed, such as in places and seasons where the sun does not rise or does not set, the zman may not be available.

name: getSofZmanShmaGRA

user_docs.meaning: The latest time to recite morning Shema according to the Vilna Gaon (GRA).
user_docs.calculation: It is 3 seasonal hours after sunrise, using the GRA day length from sunrise to sunset.
notes: {uses_elevation}
The day is measured from sea level sunrise to sea level sunset, or from sunrise to sunset, depending on whether elevation is used.
If the sun does not rise or does not set on a given day, this zman may not be available.

name: getSofZmanShmaMGA72Minutes

user_docs.meaning: The latest time to recite the morning Shema according to the Magen Avraham, using dawn that is 72 minutes before sunrise.
user_docs.calculation: It is 3 shaos zmaniyos after alos, where alos is 72 minutes before sunrise. This same day is treated as running from 72-minute dawn to 72-minute nightfall.
notes: If the sun does not rise or set on a given day, this zman may not be available and cannot be calculated.
This follows the Magen Avraham view of the day based on 72-minute dawn and 72-minute nightfall.

name: getTzais72Minutes
docs: This method returns the tzais (nightfall) based on the opinion of Rabbeinu Tam that tzais hakochavim is calculated as 72 minutes after sunset, the time it takes to walk 4 mil at 18 minutes a mil. According to the Machtzis Hashekel in Orach Chaim 235:3, the Pri Megadim in Orach Chaim 261:2 (see the Biur Halacha) and others (see Hazmanim Bahalacha 17:3 and 17:5) the 72 minutes are standard clock minutes any time of the year in any location. Depending on the {@link isUseElevation()} setting, a 72-minute offset from either {@link getSunset() sunset} or {@link getSeaLevelSunset() sea level sunset} is used. @return the Instant representing 72 minutes after sunset. If the calculation can't be computed such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year where the sun does not rise, and one where it does not set, a null will be returned See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation. @see ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar#getTzais16Point1Degrees()

user_docs.meaning: Nightfall based on Rabbeinu Tam's view that tzais hakochavim is 72 minutes after sunset.
user_docs.calculation: Calculated as a 72-minute offset after sunset.
notes: {uses_elevation}
{sea_level}
The 72 minutes are treated as standard clock minutes any time of year and in any location.
The zman may not be available where the sun does not rise or does not set, such as in the Arctic Circle.

name: getCandleLighting

user_docs.meaning: The candle lighting time, for any day of the week, including mid-week holidays.
user_docs.calculation: Sea level sunset minus {candel_lighting_offset} minutes.
notes: Elevation adjustments are intentionally not performed.
If the sun does not rise or set on a given day, the zman may not be available.

name: getSofZmanTfilaGRA
docs: This method returns the latest zman tfila (time to recite shema in the morning) that is 4 * {@link getShaahZmanisGRA() shaos zmaniyos }(solar hours) after {@link getSunrise() sunrise} or {@link getSeaLevelSunrise() sea level sunrise} (depending on the {@link isUseElevation()} setting), according to the GRA. The day is calculated from {@link getSeaLevelSunrise() sea level sunrise} to {@link getSeaLevelSunset() sea level sunset} or from {@link getSunrise() sunrise} to {@link getSunset() sunset} (depending on the {@link isUseElevation()} setting). @see getSofZmanTfila(Instant, Instant) @see getShaahZmanisGRA() @see ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar#getSofZmanTfilaBaalHatanya() @return the Instant of the latest zman tfilah. If the calculation can't be computed such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year where the sun does not rise, and one where it does not set, a null will be returned. See detailed explanation on top of the {@link AstronomicalCalendar} documentation.

user_docs.meaning: The latest zman tfila, based on the GRA, for reciting morning shema.
user_docs.calculation: It is 4 shaos zmaniyos after sunrise, using either sunrise to sunset or sea level sunrise to sea level sunset depending on whether elevation is used.
notes: The calculation depends on the elevation setting: it uses sunrise and sunset when elevation is used, and sea level sunrise and sea level sunset otherwise.
In locations such as the Arctic Circle, where the sun may not rise or set on some days, the zman may not be available.

name: getSofZmanTfilaMGA72Minutes

user_docs.meaning: The latest time for morning shema according to the Magen Avraham approach, based on a day measured from 72 minutes before sunrise to 72 minutes after sunset.
user_docs.calculation: It is 4 shaos zmaniyos after alos 72 minutes, using the day's length from 72 minutes before sunrise to 72 minutes after sunset.
notes: The use of elevation depends on the isUseElevation setting. {uses_elevation}
In places and on days where the sun does not rise or does not set, the zman may not be available or cannot be calculated.

name: getMinchaGedolaGRA

user_docs.meaning: The latest mincha gedola, which is the earliest time one may pray mincha according to the GRA.
user_docs.calculation: It is 6.5 shaos zmaniyos after sunrise, or after sea level sunrise if elevation is not used. The day is measured from sunrise to sunset, or from sea level sunrise to sea level sunset if elevation is not used.
notes: This is the GRA-based time for the earliest mincha.
The zman may not be available in places or times where sunrise and sunset cannot be calculated, such as parts of the Arctic Circle.
The Rambam prefers delaying mincha until mincha ketana GRA, while the Rash, Tur, GRA, and others allow mincha starting at mincha gedola.

name: getMinchaKetanaGRA

user_docs.meaning: The preferred earliest time to pray mincha, known as mincha ketana, according to the Rambam and others. It is set at 9.5 seasonal hours after sunrise.
user_docs.calculation: Calculated as 9.5 seasonal hours after sunrise, using the GRA day from sunrise to sunset.
notes: {uses_elevation}
If elevation is not used, the day is measured from sea level sunrise to sea level sunset. If elevation is used, the day is measured from sunrise to sunset.
In locations such as the Arctic Circle, where the sun does not rise on one day and does not set on another, the time may not be available.

name: getPlagHaminchaGRA

user_docs.meaning: The time of plag hamincha according to the GRA. It is the earliest time that Shabbos can be started.
user_docs.calculation: It is calculated as 10.75 shaos zmaniyos after sunrise, using either sunrise and sunset or sea level sunrise and sea level sunset, depending on the elevation setting.
notes: {uses_elevation}
If the sun does not rise or set on a given day, the zman may not be available.
